title: Cost Allocation Rules in Minimum Cost Spanning Tree Problems
text: goal mcstprules provide comprehensive set tools compute, analyze, visualize cost allocation rules Minimum Cost Spanning Tree Problems (MCSTP). package implements algorithmic methods, rules based cooperative game theory, robust suite analytical tools.

title: Cost Allocation Rules in Minimum Cost Spanning Tree Problems

code: # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("luciasouto13/mcstprules") library(mcstprules)

title: Cost Allocation Rules in Minimum Cost Spanning Tree Problems
text: package organizes functions three main categories:

title: Cost Allocation Rules in Minimum Cost Spanning Tree Problems
text: rules derived directly network’s structure classic MST algorithms: Prim-Based: mcstBird (Bird) mcstDuttaKar (Dutta-Kar). Kruskal-Based: mcstFolk (Folk/ERO), mcstOWShapley (Optimistic Weighted Shapley), mcstPWShapley (Pessimistic Weighted Shapley). Approaches: mcstBoruvka (Boruvka) mcstCone (Cone-wise Decomposition).

title: Cost Allocation Rules in Minimum Cost Spanning Tree Problems
text: rules compute Shapley value Nucleolus cooperative games associated MCSTP: mcstGamePrivate: also known pessimistic game. Shapley value corresponds Kar’s rule (mcstKar). mcstGameIrred: based irreducible cost matrix. mcstGameOpt: optimistic game approach. mcstGamePublic: connections agents publicly available. mcstGameCC: based cycle-complete matrix.

title: Cost Allocation Rules in Minimum Cost Spanning Tree Problems
text: comprehensive suite evaluate stability sensitivity: Core Stability & Geometry: mcstCoreCheck, mcstCorePoints, mcstCorePlot evaluate stability, find blocking coalitions, visualize core region. Sensitivity: mcstStabilityRange mcstSensitivity evaluate cost variations affect optimal trees cost allocations. Comparison: mcstCompare simultaneously evaluate main rules.

title: Cost Allocation Rules in Minimum Cost Spanning Tree Problems
text: package highly flexible accepts network costs multiple formats: square matrices, lower triangular vectors, edge lists, igraph objects, even direct .csv .xlsx file paths. basic example shows define cost matrix compute cost allocations 3-agent problem (node 0 source):
code: library(mcstprules)  # Define a cost matrix for 3 agents + source (node 0)  # 1. Using an edge list format costs <- data.frame(from = c(0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 2),                     to   = c(1, 2, 3, 2, 3, 3),                     cost = c(12, 15, 12, 4, 6, 8))  # 2. Using a square matrix # costs <- matrix(c(0, 12, 15, 12, #                  12,  0,  4,  6, #                  15,  4,  0,  8, #                  12,  6,  8,  0), nrow = 4, byrow = TRUE)  # 3. Using the lower triangular vector # costs <- c(12, 15, 12, 4, 6, 8)    # Algorithmic rules mcstBird(costs) #> Non-unique MCST detected #>           1 2 3 #> B(id)    12 4 6 #> E[B(pi)]  9 4 9 mcstFolk(costs) #> 1 2 3  #> 7 7 8  # Cooperative game theory rules mcstKar(costs) #>       S v^p(S) #> 1   {1}     12 #> 2   {2}     15 #> 3   {3}     12 #> 4 {1,2}     16 #> 5 {1,3}     18 #> 6 {2,3}     20 #> 7     N     22 #>  #> Solution: Shapley  #>    1    2    3  #> 5.83 8.33 7.83 mcstGamePublic(costs, sol = "nucleolus") #>       S v^u(S) #> 1   {1}     12 #> 2   {2}     15 #> 3   {3}     12 #> 4 {1,2}     16 #> 5 {1,3}     18 #> 6 {2,3}     20 #> 7     N     22 #>  #> Solution: Nucleolus  #>  1  2  3  #> 10 13 -1

title: Bird's Rule for MCSTP — mcstBird
text: function computes Bird's cost allocation (Bird, 1976) minimum cost spanning tree problem \((N_0, C)\). Bird rule, denoted \(B(N_0, C)\), assigns agent cost arc connects source construction minimum cost spanning tree using Prim's algorithm.

title: Bird's Rule for MCSTP — mcstBird
code: mcstBird(   C,   draw = FALSE,   which.plot = c("main", "details"),   titles = TRUE,   method = c("exact", "montecarlo"),   nsim = 10000 )

title: Bird's Rule for MCSTP — mcstBird
text: C cost matrix nodes. Accepts multiple formats (see "Supported formats C" ). draw logical; TRUE, plots network highlighting optimal tree red, indicating stage arc added (brackets) cost allocated agent (parentheses node). \(n \le 3\) ties method = "exact", also displays possible trees. .plot character string indicating plots display (\(n \le 3\) ties method = "exact"). "details", displays detailed breakdown trees allocations according agents' entry orders; "main", average allocation plotted. Default c("main", "details"). titles logical; TRUE (default), adds main title specifying allocation rule subtitle algorithm, method (exact Monte Carlo), total network cost. method character string specifying calculation method ties occur. "exact" (default), computes \(n!\) permutations; "montecarlo", estimates average allocation random sampling using nsim permutations (recommended large networks). nsim integer; number permutations sample using method = "montecarlo". Default 10,000.

title: Bird's Rule for MCSTP — mcstBird
text: list containing: bird: Bird's allocation vector \(B(N_0, C)\) natural ordering. e_bird: extended Bird's allocation (average permutations ties exist; equal bird otherwise). arcs: MCST unique, data frame edges \((, j)\) tree stage added; ties exist, list data frames, one per distinct optimal tree. total: total cost MCST, \(m(N_0, C)\). percentage: share total cost allocated agent, based e_bird. ranking: ranking agents cost e_bird (highest lowest; ties marked *). perms: ties exist, list $table (allocations per permutation, first 6 rows shown \(n>3\)), $summary (distinct allocations multiplicity) $nperms (total number permutations computed); NULL MCST unique. is_unique: logical; TRUE, MCST unique. method: method applied computing extended rule ("exact" "montecarlo").

title: Bird's Rule for MCSTP — mcstBird
text: given tree \(g^n\) constructed following Prim's algorithm, allocation agent \(\N\) given : $$B_i(N_0, C) = c_{^0 },$$ \(^0\) node agent \(\) first connected process building network. minimum cost spanning tree unique, rule extended averaging allocations possible permutations \(\pi \\Pi_N\) (method = "exact"): $$B(N_0, C) = \displaystyle{\dfrac{1}{n!} \sum_{\pi \\Pi_N} B^{\pi}(N_0, C)},$$ \(B^{\pi}(N_0, C)\) allocation obtained applying Prim's algorithm \((N_0, C)\) solving indifferences selecting first agent given permutation \(\pi\), proposed Dutta Kar (2004). Alternatively, method = "montecarlo" estimates average allocation random sampling using \(n_{\text{sim}}\) permutations. highly useful number agents large, significantly reduces computation time providing accurate approximation: $$B(N_0, C) \approx \displaystyle{\dfrac{1}{n_{\text{sim}}} \sum_{k=1}^{n_{\text{sim}}} B^{\pi_k}(N_0, C)},$$ \(\pi_k\) represents randomly sampled permutations. networks agents, exact method preferred avoid potential overestimations.

title: Bird's Rule for MCSTP — mcstBird
code: # Simple vector input mcstBird(c(12, 15, 20, 4, 6, 8), draw = TRUE)  #>  1  2  3  #> 12  4  6   # Input with infinite costs (disconnected nodes) C_inf <- c(5, 9, Inf, 15, 6, Inf, 7, Inf, Inf, Inf,            Inf, 8, 7, Inf, Inf, 5, Inf, Inf, 8, 9, 11) mcstBird(C_inf) #> 1 2 3 4 5 6  #> 5 7 5 7 6 9   # Matrix input with ties C_mat <- matrix(c(0, 12, 15, 12,                  12,  0,  4,  6,                  15,  4,  0,  8,                  12,  6,  8,  0), byrow = TRUE, ncol = 4) mcstBird(C_mat, draw = TRUE, which.plot = "main", titles = FALSE)  #> Non-unique MCST detected #>           1 2 3 #> B(id)    12 4 6 #> E[B(pi)]  9 4 9

title: Boruvka's Rule for MCSTP — mcstBoruvka
text: function computes Boruvka's cost allocation (Bergantiños Vidal- Puga, 2011) minimum cost spanning tree problem \((N_0, C)\). Boruvka's rule, denoted \(\beta^{\pi}(N_0, C)\), defined Boruvka's algorithm.

title: Boruvka's Rule for MCSTP — mcstBoruvka
code: mcstBoruvka(C, draw = FALSE, titles = TRUE)

title: Boruvka's Rule for MCSTP — mcstBoruvka
text: C cost matrix nodes. Accepts multiple formats (see "Supported formats C" ). draw logical; TRUE, plots network highlighting optimal tree red, indicating stage arc added (brackets) cost allocated agent (parentheses node). titles logical; TRUE (default), adds main title specifying allocation rule subtitle algorithm used total network cost.

title: Boruvka's Rule for MCSTP — mcstBoruvka
text: list containing: boruvka: Boruvka's allocation vector \(\beta^{\pi}(N_0, C)\). arcs: data frame edges \((, j)\) final tree stage added. total: total cost MCST, \(m(N_0, C)\). percentage: share total cost allocated agent. ranking: ranking agents cost (highest lowest; ties marked *). is_unique: logical; TRUE, MCST unique.

title: Boruvka's Rule for MCSTP — mcstBoruvka
text: Boruvka allocation divides cost optimal tree step--step process \(\gamma\) stages. stage \(s\), agents pay maximum possible proportion \(p^s\) cheapest arc associated connected component. Let \(^s\) set non-completely paid arcs, \(\varrho_{ij}^s\) proportion cost arc \((,j)\) already paid, \(N_{ij}^s\) set agents paying arc \((,j)\) stage \(s\). proportion \(p^s\) given : $$p^s = \min \left\{ \dfrac{1 - \varrho_{ij}^{s-1}}{|N_{ij}^s|} : (,j) \^{s-1}, ~ N_{ij}^s \neq \emptyset \right\}.$$ Let \(a_i^s\) arc partially paid agent \(\) stage \(s\). cost paid agent \(\) stage : $$f_i^s = p^s c_{a_i^s}.$$ process finishes \(\gamma\) stages tree completely paid, .e., \(\sum_{s=1}^\gamma p^s = 1\). Boruvka allocation agent \(\N\) given : $$\beta_i^{\pi}(N_0, C) = \displaystyle{\sum_{s=1}^\gamma f_i^s.}$$

title: Boruvka's Rule for MCSTP — mcstBoruvka
text: Boruvka's allocation uniquely determined even minimum cost spanning tree unique. Since rule depends evolution connected components rather specific arcs selected, tie-breaking selection Boruvka's algorithm yields cost distribution. Furthermore, demonstrated Bergantiños Vidal-Puga (2011), order \(\pi\) used algorithm, Boruvka's allocation equivalent mcstFolk.

what: Cone-wise Decomposition Rules for MCSTP — mcstCone
title: Cone-wise Decomposition Rules for MCSTP — mcstCone
text: function computes cost allocations minimum cost spanning tree problems \((N_0, C)\) using cone-wise decomposition introduced Norde et al. (2004). technique allows extending rules defined elementary MCSTPs MCSTP.

title: Cone-wise Decomposition Rules for MCSTP — mcstCone
code: mcstCone(   C,   rule = c("folk", "owshapley", "bogomolnaia"),   weights = NULL,   lambda = 1 )

title: Cone-wise Decomposition Rules for MCSTP — mcstCone
text: C cost matrix nodes. Accepts multiple formats (see "Supported formats C" ). rule character string indicating rule \(R\) applied elementary MCSTPs. One "folk" (default), "owshapley", "bogomolnaia". weights numeric vector strictly positive weights agent \(N\). length vector must match number agents; rule = "owshapley". lambda non-negative numeric parameter (1 default); rule = "bogomolnaia".

title: Cone-wise Decomposition Rules for MCSTP — mcstCone
text: list containing: allocation: cost allocation vector \(R(N_0, C)\) chosen rule \(R\). rule: rule \(R\) applied allocation. total: total cost MCST, \(m(N_0, C)\). percentage: share total cost allocated agent. ranking: ranking agents cost (highest lowest; ties marked *). decomposition: data frame containing details cone-wise decomposition, including cost threshold, weight layer elementary allocation agent. weights: weight vector used allocation; rule = "owshapley". lambda: lambda value used allocation; rule = "bogomolnaia".

title: Cone-wise Decomposition Rules for MCSTP — mcstCone
text:
MCSTP can written nonnegative combination elementary MCST \(C = \sum_{q=1}^{m(C)} x^q C^q\), costs arcs \(C^q\) 0 1. rule \(R\) extended : $$R(N_0, C) = \displaystyle{\sum_{q=1}^{m(C)} x^q R(N_0, C^q).}$$ Let \(g^q\) network stage \(q \{1, \dots, m(C)\}\), \(S_i^q := S(P(g^q), )\) connected component containing agent \(\) graph induced zero-cost arcs. elementary problem \((N_0, C^q)\), agents component \(S\) containing source (0) divide cost connecting source according chosen rule: Folk rule ("folk"): $$F(N_0, C^q) =  egin{cases}    \dfrac{1}{|S_i^q|} & 	ext{} 0 
otin S_i^q \    0 & 	ext{otherwise}.    \end{cases}$$ Optimistic weighted Shapley rule ("owshapley"): $$f^{\varrho^{ow}}(N_0, C^q) =  egin{cases}    \dfrac{w_i}{\sum_{j \S_i^q} w_j} & 	ext{} 0 
otin S_i^q \    0 & 	ext{otherwise}.    \end{cases}$$ Bogomolnaia Moulin family ("bogomolnaia"): \(\lambda \[0, +\infty)\),    $$R^\lambda(N_0, C^q) =  egin{cases}    \dfrac{\lambda^{\delta_i}}{\sum_{j \S_i^q} \lambda^{\delta_j}} & 	ext{} 0 
otin S_i^q \    0 & 	ext{otherwise},    \end{cases}$$   \(\delta_i\) denotes number non-null arcs \(C^q\) containing   agent \(\). \(\lambda = +\infty\),    $$R^\lambda(N_0, C^q) =  egin{cases}    \arg\max_{j \S_i^q} \delta_j & 	ext{} 0 
otin S_i^q \    0 & 	ext{otherwise},    \end{cases}$$    .e., agents attaining maximum value \(\delta_j\) share cost equally.

title: Cone-wise Decomposition Rules for MCSTP — mcstCone
text: function focuses analytical numerical decomposition cost allocation. graphical representations, use direct rule functions (e.g., mcstFolk). allocation rule = "folk" also obtained weights equal rule = "owshapley", \(\lambda = 1\) rule = "bogomolnaia".

what: Core Stability Check for Cost Allocations — mcstCoreCheck
title: Core Stability Check for Cost Allocations — mcstCoreCheck
text: function evaluates whether given cost allocation belongs core cooperative game. checks efficiency coalitional rationality, identifying blocking coalitions allocation unstable.

title: Core Stability Check for Cost Allocations — mcstCoreCheck
code: mcstCoreCheck(game = NULL, allocation = NULL, v = NULL, tol = 1e-07)  # S3 method for class 'mcstp_core' plot(x, col = "firebrick", titles = TRUE, ...)

title: Core Stability Check for Cost Allocations — mcstCoreCheck
text: game list; object containing cooperative game computed method = "exact" (e.g., mcstGamePrivate). Default NULL. allocation numeric vector; proposed cost allocation agent. Required game NULL. v numeric vector; characteristic function values \(v(S)\) \(2^n - 1\) coalitions, ordered cardinality. Required game NULL. tol numeric; tolerance floating-point comparisons avoid precision issues. Default 1e-7. x output mcstCoreCheck. col character string specifying color excess bars. Default "firebrick". titles logical; TRUE (default), adds main title y-axis label plot. ... additional graphical parameters passed barplot.

title: Core Stability Check for Cost Allocations — mcstCoreCheck
text: list containing: in_core: logical; TRUE, allocation stable (belongs core). is_efficient: logical; TRUE, sum allocation equals total cost. is_rational: logical; TRUE, blocking coalitions exist. blocking_coals: data frame detailing blocking coalitions \(S\), allocated cost \(x(S)\), value \(v(S)\), excess. Empty allocation rational. n: number agents. sum_x: sum allocated costs. total: total cost game \(v(N)\).

title: Core Stability Check for Cost Allocations — mcstCoreCheck
text: allocation \(x \\mathbb{R}^N\) belongs core cooperative game \((N, v)\) satisfies two fundamental conditions: Efficiency: total cost completely allocated among agents, .e., $$\sum_{\N} x_i = v(N).$$ Coalitional rationality: coalition pays stand-alone cost, .e., $$\sum_{\S} x_i \le v(S) \quad \forall S \subset N.$$ coalitional rationality condition violated coalition \(S\), \(S\) considered blocking coalition, members incentive leave grand coalition form sub-network. excess \(x(S) - v(S)\) quantifies magnitude violation. function can executed two ways: passing complete game object (must computed method = "exact"), manually providing proposed allocation vector characteristic function v. plot method visualizes excess blocking coalitions () using bar chart.

title: Core Stability Check for Cost Allocations — mcstCoreCheck
text: Bergantiños G, Vidal-Puga J (2021) review cooperative rules associated algorithms minimum-cost spanning tree problems. SERIEs, 12:73-100

title: Core Stability Check for Cost Allocations — mcstCoreCheck
code: # Example 1: Using manual vectors vvals <- c(10, 12, 15, 20, 22, 25, 30) # 3 agents: v(1), v(2), v(3), v(12), ... # Stable check alloc1 <- c(8, 10, 12) check1 <- mcstCoreCheck(allocation = alloc1, v = vvals); check1 #> ------------------------- #>  Core Stability Analysis #> ------------------------- #> Players (n) : 3  #> Efficiency  : Passed (Sum x: 30.00 | v(N): 30.00) #> Rationality : Passed  #>  #> Result: the allocation IS IN THE CORE (Stable) # Unstable check alloc2 <- c(15, 10, 5) check2 <- mcstCoreCheck(allocation = alloc2, v = vvals); check2 #> ------------------------- #>  Core Stability Analysis #> ------------------------- #> Players (n) : 3  #> Efficiency  : Passed (Sum x: 30.00 | v(N): 30.00) #> Rationality : Failed  #>  #> Result: the allocation IS NOT IN THE CORE (Unstable) #>  #> Blocking Coalitions (x(S) > v(S)): #>      S x(S) v(S) excess #>    {1}   15   10      5 #>  {1,2}   25   20      5 plot(check2)  # \donttest{ # Example 2: Using a game object C <- matrix(c(0, 12, 15, 12, 0, 4, 15, 4, 0), byrow = TRUE, ncol = 3) # Check if the Shapley value of the private game is in the core mcstCoreCheck(game = mcstGamePrivate(C)) #> ------------------------- #>  Core Stability Analysis #> ------------------------- #> Players (n) : 2  #> Efficiency  : Passed (Sum x: 16.00 | v(N): 16.00) #> Rationality : Passed  #>  #> Result: the allocation IS IN THE CORE (Stable) # }

what: Plot the Core Region and Cost Allocations — mcstCorePlot

title: Plot the Core Region and Cost Allocations — mcstCorePlot
text: game list; object containing cooperative game (e.g., mcstGamePrivate). provided allocations empty, allocation solution concept used default. Default NULL. v numeric vector; characteristic function values \(v(S)\) \(2^n - 1\) coalitions, ordered cardinality. Required game NULL. allocations list; named list numeric vectors representing different cost allocations plotted points. Default list(). titles logical; TRUE (default), adds main title subtitle/legend information plot.

title: Plot the Core Region and Cost Allocations — mcstCorePlot
text: \(n = 2\) \(n = 3\), function draws base R plot. \(n = 4\), returns interactive plotly object containing 3D visualization.

title: Plot the Core Region and Cost Allocations — mcstCorePlot
text: geometric representation core imputation set depends number players \(n\): \(n = 2\) players: imputation set represented 1D line segment \(x_1 + x_2 = v(N)\). core highlighted sub-segment within , delimited individual rationality. \(n = 3\) players: allocations projected 2D barycentric coordinates (simplex). imputation set forms large triangle, core plotted inside bounded convex polygon. \(n = 4\) players: function switches interactive 3D plot using plotly package. imputation set drawn transparent 3D tetrahedron (mesh), core represented solid 3D polytope inside . allocation lies inside green core region, means allocation coalitionally stable (group players incentives defect). function automatically extracts default allocation game object passed. Alternatively, custom named list allocations can provided compare different rules (e.g., Bird, Shapley value, folk) graph.

title: Check Core Emptiness — mcstCorePoints
text: problem finding cost allocation \(x \\mathbb{R}^N\) core formulated Linear Programming system. allocation must satisfy two fundamental conditions: Efficiency: total cost completely allocated among agents, .e., $$\sum_{\N} x_i = v(N).$$ Coalitional rationality: coalition pays stand-alone cost, .e., $$\sum_{\S} x_i \le v(S) \quad \forall S \subset N.$$ function uses lpSolve package evaluate system equations inequalities. solution exists, core non-empty feasible allocation returned. Furthermore, games 6 players (\(n \le 6\)), function automatically utilizes rcdd package perform vertex enumeration. converts system linear inequalities exact geometric vertices core. particularly useful analyzing bounds core region. plot method provides visualization core computed feasible point internally calling mcstCorePlot. supported games 2, 3, 4 players.

title: Dutta-Kar's Rule for MCSTP — mcstDuttaKar

Accepts multiple formats (see "Supported formats C" ). draw logical; TRUE, plots network highlighting optimal tree red, indicating stage arc added (brackets) cost allocated agent (parentheses node). \(n \le 3\) ties method = "exact", also displays possible trees. .plot character string indicating plots display (\(n \le 3\) ties method = "exact"). "details", displays detailed breakdown trees allocations according agents' entry orders; "main", average allocation plotted. Default c("main", "details"). titles logical; TRUE (default), adds main title specifying allocation rule subtitle algorithm, method (exact Monte Carlo), total network cost. method character string specifying calculation method ties occur. "exact" (default), computes \(n!\) permutations; "montecarlo", estimates average allocation random sampling using nsim permutations (recommended large networks). nsim integer; number permutations sample using method = "montecarlo". title: Dutta-Kar's Rule for MCSTP — mcstDuttaKar
text: list containing: dk: Dutta-Kar's allocation vector \(DK(N_0, C)\) natural ordering. e_dk: extended Dutta-Kar's allocation (average permutations ties exist; equal dk otherwise). arcs: MCST unique, data frame edges \((, j)\) tree stage added; ties exist, list data frames, one per distinct optimal tree. total: total cost MCST, \(m(N_0, C)\). percentage: share total cost allocated agent, based e_dk. ranking: ranking agents cost e_dk (highest lowest; ties marked *). perms: ties exist, list $table (allocations per permutation, first 6 rows shown \(n>3\)), $summary (distinct allocations multiplicity) $nperms (total number permutations computed); NULL MCST unique. is_unique: logical; TRUE, MCST unique. method: method applied computing extended rule ("exact" "montecarlo").

title: Dutta-Kar's Rule for MCSTP — mcstDuttaKar
text: Following Prim's algorithm, let \(x^0 = 0\). stage \(p \\{1, \dots, n\}\), algorithm selects minimum cost arc \((^p, j^p)\) connects new agent \(j^p\) already connected set nodes. update accumulated cost : $$x^p = \max \{x^{p-1}, c_{^p j^p}\}.$$ agent \(\N\), exists stage \(p()\) \(= j^{p()}\). Dutta-Kar's allocation agent \(\N\) defined : $$DK_i(N_0, C) = \min \{x^{p()-1}, c_{^{p()} j^{p()}}\}.$$ minimum cost spanning tree unique, rule extended averaging allocations possible permutations \(\pi \\Pi_N\) (method = "exact"): $$DK(N_0, C) = \displaystyle{\frac{1}{n!} \sum_{\pi \\Pi_N} DK^{\pi}(N_0, C)},$$ \(DK^{\pi}(N_0, C)\) allocation obtained applying Prim's algorithm \((N_0, C)\) solving indifferences selecting first agent given permutation \(\pi\). Alternatively, method = "montecarlo" estimates average allocation random sampling using \(n_{\text{sim}}\) permutations. highly useful number agents large, significantly reduces computation time providing accurate approximation: $$DK(N_0, C) \approx \displaystyle{\dfrac{1}{n_{\text{sim}}} \sum_{k=1}^{n_{\text{sim}}} DK^{\pi_k}(N_0, C)},$$ \(\pi_k\) represents randomly sampled permutations. networks agents, exact method preferred avoid potential overestimations.

title: Folk Rule for MCSTP — mcstFolk
text: Following Kruskal's algorithm, let \(g^p\) network stage \(p \\{1, \dots, n\}\), \(S_i^p := S(P(g^p), )\) connected component containing agent \(\). folk rule based obligation function: $$o_i(S) = \dfrac{1}{|S|}.$$ stage \(p\), let \(c_{^p j^p}\) cost arc added network. folk allocation agent \(\N\) given : $$F_i(N_0, C) = \displaystyle{ \sum_{p=1}^n c_{^p j^p} \left( o_i(S_i^{p-1}) - o_i(S_i^p) \right)}.$$

title: Cost Allocations based on the Cycle-complete Game for MCSTP — mcstGameCC
text: cycle-complete game \((N, v_C^c)\) cooperative game transferable utility (TU game) defined coalition \(S \subseteq N\) : $$v_C^c(S) = m(S_0, C^*),$$ \(m(S_0, C^*)\) cost minimum spanning tree subproblem induced agents \(S\) source, evaluated cycle-complete costs \(C^*\). cycle-complete cost defined minimum direct cost \(c_{ij}\) smallest value maximum arc cost cycles containing \(\) \(j\), .e., \(c_{ij}^* = \min(c_{ij}, \min_{f \\mathcal{C}(,j)} \max_{e \f} c_e),\) \(\mathcal{C}(,j)\) denotes set cycles include \(\) \(j\). computational efficiency, \(C^*\) obtained via irreducible matrices \(\bar{C}\) subgraphs : $$c_{ij}^* = \max_{k \N \setminus \{,j\}} {\bar{c}}_{ij}^{N \setminus \{k\}} \text{ } ,j \N,$$ $$c_{0i}^* = \max_{k \N \setminus \{\}} {\bar{c}}_{0i}^{N \setminus \{k\}} \text{ } \N,$$ \({\bar{C}}^{N \setminus \{k\}}\) irreducible cost matrix network excluding agent \(k\). agents can removed, irreducible cost full network used. method = "exact", function builds full characteristic function (\(2^n - 1\) values). Depending chosen solution concept, computes allocation via shapleyValue (called cycle-complete solution, relies coalition-based formula) nucleolus. larger networks, method = "montecarlo" provides approximation Shapley value random sampling using \(n_{sim}\) permutations. approach implements lazy evaluation based permutation formula Shapley value: instead computing MST cost \(2^n - 1\) possible subsets, evaluates specific subcoalitions generated random permutations. highly useful number agents large, significantly reduces memory usage computation time providing accurate approximation cycle-complete solution. networks players, exact method preferred avoid potential overestimations. details formulas, see mcstRules.

title: Optimistic Weighted Shapley's Rule for MCSTP — mcstOWShapley
text: Following Kruskal's algorithm, let \(g^p\) network stage \(p \\{1, \dots, n\}\), \(S_i^p := S(P(g^p), )\) connected component containing agent \(\). optimistic weighted Shapley rule based obligation function: $$o_i(S) = \dfrac{w_i}{\sum_{j \S} w_j},$$ \(w_i > 0\) weight associated agent \(\). stage \(p\), let \(c_{^p j^p}\) cost arc added network. optimistic weighted Shapley allocation agent \(\N\) given : $$f^{\varrho^{ow}}_i(N_0, C) = \displaystyle{ \sum_{p=1}^n c_{^p j^p} \left( o_i(S_i^{p-1}) - o_i(S_i^p) \right)}.$$

title: Pessimistic Weighted Shapley's Rule for MCSTP — mcstPWShapley
text: Following Kruskal's algorithm, let \(g^p\) network stage \(p \\{1, \dots, n\}\), \(S_i^p := S(P(g^p), )\) connected component containing agent \(\). pessimistic weighted Shapley rule based obligation function: $$o_i(S) = \displaystyle{ \sum_{\pi \\Pi(S \setminus \{\})} \prod_{j=1}^{|S|-1} \dfrac{w_{\pi^{-1}(j)}}{\sum_{k=1}^{j} w_{\pi^{-1}(k)} + w_i},}$$ \(\Pi(\cdot)\) denotes set permutations given set, \(w_i > 0\) weight associated agent \(\). stage \(p\), let \(c_{^p j^p}\) cost arc added network. pessimistic weighted Shapley allocation agent \(\N\) given : $$f^{\varrho^{pw}}_i(N_0, C) = \displaystyle{ \sum_{p=1}^n c_{^p j^p} \left( o_i(S_i^{p-1}) - o_i(S_i^p) \right)}.$$

title: Overview of Rules and Analysis Tools for Minimum Cost Spanning Tree Problems — mcstRules
text: rules obtained directly network's structure classical MST algorithms. Based Prim's algorithm: rules follow growth single tree starting source. mcstBird: agents sequentially connect growing   tree, paying cost arc connect tree. mcstDuttaKar: modification Bird's rule introduces   pivotal switch allocation cost step ensure cost monotonicity. Based Kruskal's algorithm: Based obligation functions, rules follow Kruskal's logic merging connected components using cheapest available arcs. mcstFolk: agents divide cost connecting   components equally (ERO rule). mcstOWShapley: optimistic weighted Shapley rule.   generalization folk rule distributes costs proportionally   based asymmetric positive weights. mcstPWShapley: pessimistic weighted Shapley rule.   alternative weighted extension based permutation-driven obligation function. Based Boruvka's algorithm: mcstBoruvka: agents pay maximum possible proportion   cheapest arc selected component. numerical outcome   mathematically equivalent mcstFolk. Based cone-wise decomposition: mcstCone: technique decomposes general   MCSTP nonnegative combination elementary problems (costs   0 1). allows extending rules like "folk", "owshapley",   Bogomolnaia Moulin family ("bogomolnaia") general networks.

title: Overview of Rules and Analysis Tools for Minimum Cost Spanning Tree Problems — mcstRules
text: rules defined first associating cooperative game (characteristic function form \(v\)) MCSTP applying solution concept (like Shapley value Nucleolus). Cooperative games: mcstGamePrivate: defines cost coalition   MST cost subproblem formed members source (pessimistic   approach). mcstKar: defined Shapley value associated private game. mcstGameIrred: based irreducible matrix \(\bar{C}\),   costs reflect cheapest way connect pair nodes   network. mcstGameOpt: allows coalition connect nodes   outside coalition cost. mcstGamePublic: assigns coalition minimum cost   achievable connections agents publicly available. mcstGameCC: based cycle-complete matrix \(C^*\),   induces concave cooperative game. Solution concepts: Shapley value:  Based permutations: average marginal contributions   \(n!\) possible orderings (\(\Pi_N\)): $$Sh_i(N, v) = \frac{1}{n!} \sum_{\pi \\Pi_N} [v(Pre(, \pi) \cup \{\}) - v(Pre(, \pi))],$$ \(Pre(, \pi)\) set players precede agent \(\)   ordering \(\pi\). Based coalitions: weighted sum marginal contributions   possible subcoalitions \(S\) contain \(\): $$Sh_i(N, v) = \sum_{S \subseteq N \setminus \{\}} \frac{|S|! (n - |S| - 1)!}{n!} [v(S \cup \{\}) - v(S)].$$ Shapley value unique solution satisfies properties   efficiency, symmetry, additivity, null player property. Nucleolus: unique cost allocation \(x\) lexicographically minimizes   maximum excesses (complaints) coalitions. cost game, excess \(e\) coalition   \(S\) measures dissatisfaction: $$e(S, x) = \sum_{j \S} x_j - v(S).$$ nucleolus ensures dissatisfied coalition happy   possible. always efficient lies within Core Core   non-empty.

title: Overview of Rules and Analysis Tools for Minimum Cost Spanning Tree Problems — mcstRules
text: package includes comprehensive suite tools analyze stability, sensitivity, geometric properties cost allocations network: mcstCoreCheck: evaluates whether given cost allocation belongs   core cooperative game, identifying blocking coalitions unstable. mcstCorePoints: determines core game empty via   Linear Programming, computes feasible stable allocation, finds exact geometric   vertices core polytope. mcstCorePlot: generates geometric visualizations core   region imputation set 2, 3, 4 players, allowing graphical stability check. mcstStabilityRange: calculates stability range (lower   upper bounds) cost individual arcs without altering optimal tree topology. mcstSensitivity: performs sensitivity analysis varying costs   specific arcs (individually, jointly, independently) observing dynamic impact   agents' allocations. mcstCompare: computes compares cost allocations four   standard algorithmic rules (Bird, Dutta-Kar, folk, Kar) simultaneously.

title: Sensitivity Analysis for Cost Allocations — mcstSensitivity
text: sensitivity analysis can conducted three different scenarios depending structure arcs parameter independent flag: Single arc variation: single arc provided, cost varies   along designated delta interval. output plot displays evolution lines   agent's cost allocation relative absolute cost arc. Joint arc variation: multiple arcs provided   independent = FALSE, delta variation value applied   simultaneously selected arcs. Independent variation two arcs: exactly two arcs provided   independent = TRUE, grid cross-variations constructed using   delta1 delta2. output plot renders selection heatmaps   (one per agent) mapping allocations across variation space.

title: Sensitivity Analysis for Cost Allocations — mcstSensitivity
code: # Matrix input C_mat <- matrix(c(0, 10, 15, 20,                   10,  0, 25, 12,                   15, 25,  0,  8,                   20, 12,  8,  0), byrow = TRUE, ncol = 4)  # Example 1: Single arc sensitivity analysis sens_single <- mcstSensitivity(C_mat, rule = "bird", arcs = c("0", "1"), delta = c(-5, 15)) plot(sens_single)   # 2. Joint sensitivity analysis for multiple arcs arcs_list <- list(c("0", "1"), c("2", "3")) sens_joint <- mcstSensitivity(C_mat, rule = "folk", arcs = arcs_list, delta = c(-3, 3)) plot(sens_joint)
